title: The Sixth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: July 9, 2023

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon falls on the Sixth Sunday after Pentecost, placing it squarely in Ordinary Time (the long season after Pentecost extending toward Advent). Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would likely include 2 Samuel 5:1-5, 12 (David established as king), Psalm 48, Ephesians 1:3-14 (spiritual blessings in Christ), and Mark 6:14-29 (Herod's execution of John the Baptist). This particular Sunday emphasizes themes of established authority, spiritual inheritance, and the cost of faithfulness to God's word.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was navigating significant post-General Convention tensions in summer 2023, having concluded its 80th General Convention in July 2022 with decisions on LGBTQ+ inclusion that remained divisive among traditionalist congregations. By July 2023, the church was one year into implementing these resolutions while managing ongoing conversations about Anglican Communion realignment. The Diocese of Colorado (Colorado Springs-based) was operating under Bishop William Barker II, who had assumed the role in 2018, during a period of relative stability compared to national church upheaval.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing a severe heat wave in early July 2023, with temperatures approaching or exceeding 95°F for consecutive days—part of a larger pattern of extreme heat across the western United States. Locally, the city was dealing with ongoing homelessness visibility in Capitol Hill and downtown, with encampment sweeps and shelter capacity issues remaining contentious political issues under Mayor Mike Johnston's administration (inaugurated in 2023). The Colorado state legislature had recently concluded its 2023 session in May, leaving policy questions about housing and social services unresolved.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Hunter Biden federal gun trial was ongoing in Wilmington, Delaware, with conviction expected within days (July 11). Former President Donald Trump faced multiple indictments, with his classified documents case in Florida in active prosecution phase and sentencing considerations looming. The debt ceiling had been recently resolved in June 2023 after the Biden-McCarthy agreement, and inflation remained a dominant economic concern with the Federal Reserve holding interest rates steady.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Ukraine war continued with active combat in eastern regions, particularly around Bakhmut and Zaporizhzhia, as Ukraine pressed limited counteroffensive operations against Russian positions. The Turkey-Finland NATO accession process had been completed by spring 2023, but Sweden remained in limbo waiting for final Hungarian ratification, which would come in late July. The Wagner Group mutiny in Russia (June 2024) had not yet occurred, so Russian military leadership under Putin appeared externally stable, though internal tensions existed.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture in early July 2023 was dominated by the aftermath of the Hollywood actors and writers strikes, with filming and entertainment productions still recovering and schedules disrupted. The post-pandemic "return to normal" was settling into a new baseline of remote work flexibility and changed consumer habits. The general mood was one of economic anxiety mixed with relief at lower inflation trends, though political polarization remained extremely high with 2024 election campaigns already underway in media coverage.
